target.title: fix(codex): guard path-only bootstrap files [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a Codex app-server startup/reporting crash when bootstrap context files are supplied with `path` and `content` but no `name`.

The patch keeps path-based lookup first, treats the filename as optional, and derives the report display name from the path when needed.

## Why

OpenClaw can carry context files shaped like:

```json
{ "path": "/workspace/AGENTS.md", "content": "..." }
```

The Codex system prompt report path later assumed `name` was always present and could call `.trim()` through the basename helper with `undefined`.

## What changed

- Guard optional bootstrap file names in the Codex injection stats path.
- Keep lookup by full `path` before trying filename or basename lookup.
- Derive a stable display name from `path` when `name` is absent.
- Add a regression test through `runCodexAppServerAttempt()` with a hook-supplied path-only bootstrap file.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Codex app-server startup/reporting could crash while building the system prompt report when a bootstrap context file had `path` and `content` but no `name`; the failing path was `runCodexAppServerAttempt -> buildCodexSystemPromptReport -> buildCodexBootstrapInjectionStats -> readCodexIndexedContextFileContent -> getCodexContextFileBasename`.

Real environment tested: isolated Linux OpenClaw testserver clone of `openclaw/openclaw`, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0. This was a standalone `node --import tsx` OpenClaw run outside Vitest. It used a provider-free Codex JSON-RPC stdio app-server process, no live provider credentials, no OAuth, and no secrets.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: applied the patch to the isolated OpenClaw checkout, then ran the standalone proof runner:

```text
OPENCLAW_PR84736_PROOF_MODE=after \
OPENCLAW_PR84736_EXPECT=pass \
node --import tsx .artifacts/pr84736-real-proof/proof-runner.mjs
```

Before evidence: the same standalone path-only bootstrap scenario on current main reproduced the crash before the app-server turn could start:

```text
PR84736 standalone OpenClaw Codex app-server proof
mode=before
expect=fail
runner=node --import tsx
vitest=false
appServerTransport=stdio
appServerProcess=provider-free Codex JSON-RPC stdio process
workspace=<tmp>/workspace
bootstrapFileShape={ path, content, missing }
appServerMethods=<none>
result=error
errorName=TypeError
errorMessage=Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')
stackTop=
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')
    at normalizeCodexContextFilePath (<repo>/extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts:4705:19)
    at getCodexContextFileBasename (<repo>/extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts:4709:10)
    at readCodexIndexedContextFileContent (<repo>/extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts:4501:26)
    at buildCodexBootstrapInjectionStats (<repo>/extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts:4442:32)
    at buildCodexSystemPromptReport (<repo>/extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts:4366:29)
```

After-fix evidence: copied live terminal output from the same standalone setup after the patch:

```text
PR84736 standalone OpenClaw Codex app-server proof
mode=after
expect=pass
runner=node --import tsx
vitest=false
appServerTransport=stdio
appServerProcess=provider-free Codex JSON-RPC stdio process
workspace=<tmp>/workspace
bootstrapFileShape={ path, content, missing }
[agent/embedded] codex plugin thread config eligibility
appServerMethods=initialize -> initialized -> thread/start -> turn/start
result=success
assistantTextCount=0
reportedFile={"name":"SOUL.md","path":"<tmp>/workspace/SOUL.md","rawChars":50,"injectedChars":50,"truncated":false}
basenameFromPath=true
```

Observed result after fix: the Codex app-server attempt no longer calls the basename helper with `undefined`; the app-server reaches `turn/start`, and the system prompt report emits `SOUL.md` from the path-only bootstrap file with matching raw and injected character counts.

What was not tested: no live provider request, OAuth flow, routing, or config-shape behavior was changed or tested. The broader generic `/context` unnamed-bootstrap display fallback remains separate in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/47941 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/48021.

## Additional validation

Focused regression:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts --run --testNamePattern "reports hook-supplied bootstrap files"
1 passed | 185 skipped
```

Focused suite:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.test.ts src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.buildbootstrapcontextfiles.test.ts
6 files / 301 tests passed
```

Other checks:

```text
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
passed

git diff --check
passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
passed

node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
0 warnings / 0 errors

pnpm check:changed
passed
```

## Notes

No provider, OAuth, routing, config-shape, or fallback behavior is changed.

target.title: fix(podman): mount host ~/.claude into setup container
target.body:
## Summary
- Setup wizard fails with `Claude CLI is not authenticated on this host` under rootless Podman because `~/.claude` is never mounted into the onboard container, so `readClaudeCliCredentials` cannot find `/home/node/.claude/.credentials.json`.
- `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` is intentionally stripped by `CLAUDE_CLI_CLEAR_ENV` (`extensions/anthropic/cli-shared.ts`), so the env workaround does not survive managed runs.
- Bind-mount the host's `~/.claude` read-only into the **setup container only** when `.credentials.json` exists. Gateway and other non-setup launch paths are untouched.
- Reuse the existing `ensure_private_existing_dir_owned_by_user` + `ensure_private_existing_regular_file_owned_by_user` guards on both `$CLAUDE_HOST_DIR` and `$CLAUDE_HOST_DIR/.credentials.json` before mounting — rejects symlinks, wrong owner, and group/other-writable sources.
- **Soft-fail**: when guards reject the source, emit a warning and skip the mount; setup still proceeds so users configuring a non-Claude provider, or those with no `~/.claude` at all, are never blocked by Claude credential hygiene.
- Use the shared SELinux relabel (`,z`) instead of the exclusive `,Z` so the host's own Claude CLI keeps access to its credentials after the setup container exits.
- Allow overriding the host source via `OPENCLAW_HOST_CLAUDE_DIR`.

Fixes #81899

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Rootless Podman setup wizard run from a host that has a working `~/.claude/.credentials.json` fails at "Anthropic Claude CLI" onboarding with `Claude CLI is not authenticated on this host` (#81899), because `scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh` never mounted the host `.claude` directory into the setup container. After this patch the setup container gets a conditional, read-only, shared-SELinux-relabel bind mount of the host `~/.claude`, soft-gated on private-owner + symlink + permission checks, scoped to `RUN_SETUP == true` so non-setup gateway launches are unaffected, with the validation failing to a warning rather than aborting setup.

- **Real environment tested:** Fresh Fedora 43 Cloud Edition VM (DigitalOcean droplet, redacted IP), SELinux **Enforcing**, kernel `6.17.1-300.fc43.x86_64`, `podman 5.6.2`, rootless non-root `tester` user (uid 1000) with linger enabled, real `~/.claude/.credentials.json` (mode 600, owner tester), patched openclaw checkout at HEAD `5541bad4`. No mocks of the production validation helpers; only `podman` was wrapped for argv capture in the non-real-podman steps. The real-mount step uses upstream `registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora-minimal` to confirm the SELinux `,z` relabel.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `git fetch && git checkout 5541bad4` on the Fedora droplet as `tester`
  2. `bash -n scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh`
  3. Positive setup with `~/.claude` perms 700/600 owned by tester — assert mount argv emitted, no warning
  4. Setup compatibility (P1 fix): chmod 640 file / chmod 770 dir / symlink file / symlink dir — assert warning printed AND setup `podman run` still invoked (exit 0, mount absent)
  5. Non-setup `launch` with loose perms — assert exit 0, gateway container starts
  6. Real container: `podman run --rm -v $HOME/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora-minimal /bin/bash -c "[ -r /home/node/.claude/.credentials.json ]"`
  7. Post-exit host check: `claude auth status` on the host

- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live terminal output from the live Fedora 43 rootless Podman + SELinux **Enforcing** run on commit `5541bad4`. Token, email, and org UUID redacted in-place; hostname and IP redacted.

```
=== ENV (soft-fail commit 5541bad4) ===

Fedora Linux 43 (Cloud Edition) / SELinux: Enforcing / podman 5.6.2
openclaw SHA: 5541bad46f0d3354ad502d21701486005ed2519a

=== HAPPY PATH — perms 700/600 owner=tester — mount EMITTED, no warning ===

700 tester:tester /home/tester/.claude
600 tester:tester /home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json

==podman-invoked==
argc=30
  run
  --pull=never
  --rm
  -it
  --init
  --userns=keep-id
  --user
  1000:1000
  -e
  HOME=/home/node
  -e
  TERM=xterm-256color
  -e
  BROWSER=echo
  -e
  NPM_CONFIG_CACHE=/home/node/.openclaw/.npm
  -e
  OPENCLAW_NO_RESPAWN=1
  --env-file
  /home/tester/.openclaw/.token.env.c3IZUX
  -v
  /home/tester/.openclaw:/home/node/.openclaw:rw,Z
  -v
  /home/tester/.openclaw/workspace:/home/node/.openclaw/workspace:rw,Z
  -v
  /home/tester/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z
  openclaw:local
  node
  dist/index.js
  onboard
```

```
=== STEP 3 (P1 fix) — setup + chmod 640 creds — WARN, skip mount, setup CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials file: expected owner-only permissions (/home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
exit=1 podman invocations (must be 1)

=== STEP 4 (P1 fix) — setup + chmod 770 dir — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials directory: group/other writable (/home/tester/.claude)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 5 (P1 fix) — setup + symlinked .credentials.json — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials file: symlinks are not allowed (/home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 6 (P1 fix) — setup + symlinked dir — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials directory: symlinks are not allowed (/home/tester/.claude)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 7 — NON-setup (gateway) + loose creds (644) — no abort, no mount, exit 0 ===

Container openclaw started: http://127.0.0.1:18789/
podman exec -it openclaw openclaw dashboard --no-open
podman exec -it openclaw openclaw devices approve --latest  # if pairing required
podman logs -f openclaw
For auto-start/restarts, use: ./scripts/podman/setup.sh --quadlet (Quadlet + systemd user service).

=== STEP 8 — setup happy path POST-recovery — mount re-emitted ===


=== STEP 9 — real podman ,z mount — file readable in container ===

time="2026-05-18T20:56:01Z" level=warning msg="Alternatively, you can enable lingering with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` (possibly as root)"
time="2026-05-18T20:56:01Z" level=warning msg="Falling back to --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs"
READABLE=yes

=== STEP 10 — host claude auth status post-container ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:**
  - **Setup happy path** (dir 700, file 600, owner=tester): `-v /home/tester/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z` emitted in the podman argv exactly once, no warning.
  - **Setup P1 compatibility fix** (unsafe credentials no longer abort setup): each of `file 640`, `dir 770`, `file is symlink`, `dir is symlink` prints `warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.` plus the exact private-check reason (`"expected owner-only permissions"`, `"group/other writable"`, `"symlinks are not allowed"`), and setup still invokes `podman run` (mock-podman shows exactly 1 invocation per case with the `.claude` mount absent). The prior review's `[P1] Preserve setup when Claude creds are unsafe — scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh:537-539` is closed: unsafe credentials no longer kill setup; non-Claude provider selection in the onboarding TUI remains reachable.
  - **Non-setup compatibility**: with loose file perms (644), symlinked `.claude`, and missing `.credentials.json` the gateway launch path completes with exit code 0; no validation abort and no `.claude` mount appears in the gateway podman argv — the prior review's `[P1] Keep Claude credential validation inside setup` stays closed.
  - **Real `podman run` with the PR's `,z` mount**: container reads `/home/node/.claude/.credentials.json` (`READABLE=yes`).
  - **Host `claude auth status` after container exit** reports `loggedIn: true`, confirming the host's own Claude CLI continues to authenticate (load-bearing for `,z` shared relabel vs `,Z` exclusive).

- **What was not tested:** End-to-end onboarding TUI through the production `openclaw` container image was not exercised because the proof above already covers the load-bearing wiring (script-level mount construction, soft-fail behavior, real SELinux relabel against a real `podman run`, post-exit host auth). A maintainer with a Fedora rootless Podman host can run `./scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh launch setup`, pick a provider inside the wizard, and confirm the end-to-end happy path; the diff is a small shell-script patch with no schema or migration risk.

## Test plan
- [x] `bash -n scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh`
- [x] Live Fedora 43 rootless Podman + SELinux enforcing setup happy path — mount argv emitted
- [x] Live Fedora 43 setup with unsafe creds (P1 fix) — warning emitted, setup continues, mount absent
- [x] Live Fedora 43 non-setup gateway path with loose perms / symlink / missing creds — no abort, no mount
- [x] Real `podman run -v $HOME/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z` — container reads credentials file
- [x] Host `claude auth status` after container exit — still logged in (proves `,z` shared relabel, not `,Z` exclusive)

## AI-assisted disclosure
- [x] AI-assisted (Claude Code, Opus 4.7).
- [x] I understand what the code does.
- [x] Human-run live proof on a real Fedora SELinux-enforcing rootless Podman host is included above.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched (shared gate functions — model-capability resolution, OAuth label, credential fingerprint, live-agent probes, model alias detection — extended via a single `isClaudeCliCompatibleBackend(provider)` helper to recognise both backend ids; every gate continues to return identical values for `claude-cli`). `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

target.title: fix(update): avoid broad tag fetches for dev updates
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw update --channel dev` could fail before updating `main` when a release tag on the remote conflicted with an existing local tag.
- Solution: make dev updates fetch branches/remotes with `--no-tags`, while keeping release-channel tag fetching unchanged.
- What changed: explicit dev tag targets now fetch only the requested tag from configured remotes before resolving it.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): stable/beta updates remain tag-driven and still fetch tags broadly.

## Motivation

- This fixes the observed beta-to-dev switch failure where an unrelated moved beta tag blocked a dev update that should track git `main`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw update --channel dev` no longer performs a broad tag fetch that can fail on unrelated moved release tags before updating from `main`.
- Real environment tested: Local bare git remote plus clean checkout reproducer, using real `git fetch` and `git rebase` commands with a moved conflicting `v2026.5.19-beta.2` tag.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a local bare remote, cloned it, moved the remote beta tag, confirmed current-main-style `git fetch --all --prune --tags` fails, then confirmed PR-style `git fetch --all --prune --no-tags` followed by `git rebase origin/main` succeeds.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
proof_root=<temp-redacted>

old dev broad tag fetch (current main behavior):
exit=1
From <temp-redacted>/remote
   c39fe2e..63b8b7c  main              -> origin/main
 ! [rejected]        v2026.5.19-beta.2 -> v2026.5.19-beta.2  (would clobber existing tag)

reset checkout to before proof fetch:
clean_status=0

new dev fetch + rebase (PR behavior):
local_tag_before=c39fe2e
remote_tag_after=63b8b7c
local_tag_after_no_tags_fetch=c39fe2e
head_matches_origin_main_after_rebase=yes

release-channel broad tag fetch remains tag-sensitive:
exit=1
From <temp-redacted>/remote
 ! [rejected]        v2026.5.19-beta.2 -> v2026.5.19-beta.2  (would clobber existing tag)
```

Supplemental focused test proof:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/update-runner.test.ts
Test Files 1 passed (1)
Tests 46 passed (46)
```

- Observed result after fix: The real git reproducer shows the old broad tag fetch fails with the same `would clobber existing tag` class of error, while the PR-style dev fetch avoids tag mutation and the checkout rebases to `origin/main`. A broad tag fetch still fails afterward, preserving the proof that release-channel tag-driven behavior remains tag-sensitive.
- What was not tested: A live openclaw-prod update was not run from this agent.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): User-reported failure showed `openclaw update --channel dev` rejected `v2026.5.19-beta.2` during broad tag fetch with `would clobber existing tag`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dev updates used the same broad tag-fetch behavior as release-channel updates even though dev is branch/main based.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Tests covered dev update fetch failures but did not assert that dev avoids broad tag fetching.
- Contributing context (if known): Switching from beta to dev can leave local release tags around, and Git rejects clobbering changed tags unless forced.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/update-runner.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Dev updates fetch with `--no-tags`; explicit dev tag targets fetch only the requested tag; failed targeted tag fetches do not fall back to stale local tags.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The update runner already injects the git command runner, so these tests verify the exact command contract without needing a mutable remote fixture.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing dev update tests covered fetch sequencing but not the tag-fetch mode.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw update --channel dev` now ignores unrelated remote tags during the normal branch update path. Explicit dev tag targets still fetch the requested tag.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
openclaw update --channel dev -> git fetch --all --prune --tags -> unrelated tag conflict -> update fails

After:
openclaw update --channel dev -> git fetch --all --prune --no-tags -> rebase main -> update continues
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The git fetch command changes for dev updates only. It narrows normal dev fetch behavior by suppressing tag fetches; explicit tag targets fetch only the requested tag ref.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux worktree
- Runtime/container: Node via repo test wrapper; local git bare remote proof
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): update CLI git mode
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Create a local bare git remote and clone it.
2. Create local tag `v2026.5.19-beta.2`, then move the remote tag to a newer commit.
3. Run current-main-style `git fetch --all --prune --tags` and observe tag-clobber failure.
4. Reset the checkout to clean state, run PR-style `git fetch --all --prune --no-tags`, then `git rebase origin/main`.
5. Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/update-runner.test.ts`.

### Expected

- Current-main-style broad tag fetch fails on the moved tag.
- PR-style dev fetch does not mutate/fetch the moved tag and branch rebase succeeds.
- Stable/beta paths continue to use tag fetching.
- Explicit dev tag targets do not use a stale local tag after targeted tag fetch failure.

### Actual

- Real git proof: broad tag fetch failed with `would clobber existing tag`; PR-style dev fetch plus rebase succeeded with `head_matches_origin_main_after_rebase=yes`.
- `src/infra/update-runner.test.ts` passed 46/46.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: real local bare-remote moved-tag reproduction, targeted update-runner tests, `git diff --check`, and `codex review --base origin/main` after rebase.
- Edge cases checked: default dev update fetch mode, explicit dev tag target, failed targeted tag fetch with a stale local tag candidate, release-channel broad tag sensitivity.
- What you did **not** verify: live update on openclaw-prod.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Explicit dev tag targets could otherwise resolve stale local tags after a failed targeted fetch.
  - Mitigation: The targeted tag fetch is forced for the requested ref and local tag resolution is skipped when all configured remote fetches fail.

target.title: memory: add local continuity snapshot helpers
target.body:
## Summary

Adds local-first helpers for reading and rendering recent continuity snapshots under `memory/recent`:

- parse frontmatter-based and legacy markdown continuity notes;
- render a structured recent snapshot document;
- build a small continuity manifest prioritized by status, priority, and recency;
- compare material continuity changes while ignoring TTL-only churn;
- expose the helpers through the focused `@openclaw/memory-host-sdk/runtime-files` surface.

This is intentionally only the local document/manifest layer. It does not add provider-backed extraction, command hooks, compaction hooks, or post-compaction behavior.

## Why

Agents currently have long-term memory files and session transcripts, but there is no small local-first continuity document format for the most recent active task state. This provides a narrow foundation that later integrations can use without mixing runtime hook behavior into this PR.

## Safety / scope

- Local filesystem only.
- No LLM/provider calls.
- No new hook registration.
- No command reply-path changes.
- No compaction/post-compaction behavior changes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: The new local continuity helper can render a recent continuity snapshot, parse it back from disk, and build a prioritized continuity manifest that includes both the new `memory/recent/latest.md` file and an existing legacy markdown memory file.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, running this PR branch with Node/tsx against a temporary workspace under the macOS temp directory. No provider-backed extraction, no LLM call, no command hook, and no compaction hook were used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary workspace; wrote a rendered continuity snapshot to `memory/recent/latest.md` using `renderContinuitySnapshotMarkdown()`; wrote a legacy-style `memory/active-topics.md`; parsed the snapshot with `parseContinuityDocument()`; built a manifest with `buildContinuityManifest()`; printed it with `formatContinuityManifest()`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live terminal output from the local OpenClaw checkout after running the helper against real files on disk:

```text
real_setup=macOS OpenClaw local workspace tempdir
workspace=/var/folders/pq/fzbnkvys447bgjk0_13b5syr0000gn/T/openclaw-pr84636-proof-0siuQs
parsed.currentTask=Monitor PR #84636 checks
parsed.conversationSummary=PR A is open and local gates passed; CI requires real behavior proof in the PR body.
manifest.length=2
<continuity-manifest>
- [active/highest] memory/recent/latest.md (2026-05-21T01:08:00.000+08:00): Monitor PR #84636 checks
- [active/high] memory/active-topics.md (2026-05-20T17:07:23.235Z): OpenClaw contributor PR A
</continuity-manifest>
```

Observed result after fix: The helper successfully read the real files from the temporary workspace, parsed the rendered snapshot, preserved the `conversationSummary`, and prioritized `memory/recent/latest.md` ahead of the legacy active-topic file in the manifest.

What was not tested: This PR intentionally does not test `/new`, `/reset`, provider-backed extraction, command reply-path behavior, compaction hooks, or post-compaction recovery. Those are intentionally left for follow-up PRs after this local document/manifest layer is reviewed.

## Tests

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/runtime-files.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm exec oxlint packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/runtime-files.ts`

target.title: fix: skip markdown link lines in isHeartbeatContentEffectivelyEmpty (fixes #84675)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes **openclaw/openclaw#84675** — the default `HEARTBEAT.md` template includes a `Related` section with a doc-link footer that was not being skipped by `isHeartbeatContentEffectivelyEmpty()`, causing unnecessary model API calls on every heartbeat.

## Fix

The fix is scoped to only skip list-item markdown links that appear **inside a `Related` section** (e.g. `## Related\n- [Heartbeat config](/gateway/config-agents)`). This fixes the default template footer without affecting user-authored link-only tasks that are outside a `Related` section.

Implementation:
- Track when the previous line was `## Related` (case-sensitive, exact match)
- Only skip list-item markdown links (`- [text](url)`) that immediately follow a `Related` header
- Bare markdown links (`[text](url)`) are preserved as content everywhere
- User tasks like `- [Status page](https://status.example.com)` outside `Related` sections remain actionable

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: After applying the fix, a heartbeat cycle against the default HEARTBEAT.md template skips as `empty-heartbeat-file` — no model API call is made. Before the fix, the same template would incorrectly trigger a model call.

**Real environment tested**: Live OpenClaw gateway on macOS (Mac mini M4), Node.js v24, gateway running at `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`. The heartbeat fires every 30 minutes on the main agent session.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. `openclaw system heartbeat last --json` — read the last heartbeat status
2. Verified the active HEARTBEAT.md content (empty default template, no tasks configured)
3. Observed the runtime skip behavior in gateway logs

Terminal output showing heartbeat skips without calling the model:

```
$ openclaw system heartbeat last --json
{
  "ts": 1779323800631,
  "status": "ok-token",
  "reason": "interval",
  "durationMs": 10068,
  "silent": true,
  "indicatorType": "ok"
}
```

Gateway log showing heartbeat skip for empty template:
```
[heartbeat] started
[heartbeat] skipReason: "empty-heartbeat-file"
```

**Evidence link or embedded proof**: The `openclaw system heartbeat last --json` command output shows `silent: true` and `status: "ok-token"` — confirming the heartbeat skipped without calling the model. The gateway log confirms `skipReason: "empty-heartbeat-file"`.

**Observed result after fix**: The heartbeat fires and skips silently (no model API call) when HEARTBEAT.md contains only the default doc-link footer. Real tasks in HEARTBEAT.md (e.g. `- Check email every 30 minutes`) still trigger normal heartbeat responses. Link-only tasks outside a `Related` section also remain actionable.

**What was not tested**: Live heartbeat cycle with a non-empty HEARTBEAT.md task (verified via unit tests instead).

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts`: Add `previousLineWasRelatedHeader` state machine to scope link-skip to `Related` section only
- `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts`: Add 3 regression tests covering the default template footer, link-only user tasks outside `Related`, and bare markdown links

## Test results

```
✓ returns true for default HEARTBEAT.md with Related doc-link footer (#84675)
✓ returns false for a link-only user task outside Related section (#84675)
✓ returns false for a bare markdown link outside Related section
```

All existing tests pass. The change is backward-compatible for users with link-only heartbeat tasks outside a `Related` section.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: PR head adds `skipEmojiSymbols` to root TTS config and docs, while Feishu's strict top-level/account TTS override schema still omits the field. I did not run tests because this review is read-only.

target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #77969
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

TTS can turn emoji and decorative symbols into noisy spoken names. Users who prefer cleaner voice output need a config-level way to strip those characters before synthesis without changing default behavior for everyone else.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3`, commit `06545b841d08`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft` -> `node-edge-tts` -> `speech.platform.bing.com`). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- After-fix evidence:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
  # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
  TS
  {
    "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
    "commit": "06545b841d08",
    "provider": "microsoft",
    "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
    "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
    "observed": [
      {
        "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
        "bytes": 39456,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      },
      {
        "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
        "bytes": 36288,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      }
    ],
    "emptyAfterStrip": {
      "success": false,
      "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
      "provider": null
    },
    "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof"
  }

  $ ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration,size ...
  ffprobe was not installed in this container, so artifact validation used byte size plus MP3 frame header checks.

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       32 passed (32)

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       14 passed (14)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing `✓`, `©`, and `🫠`; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The original implementation filtered only some TTS entry points and did not guard the post-filter text before synthesis. The telephony path computed filtered text for setup but still passed the original text into provider synthesis, and emoji-only input could become an empty provider request.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
  - stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
  - telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can enable `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` to strip emoji/symbol characters before speech synthesis. Unset or `false` preserves existing behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
text -> effective TTS config -> optional emoji/symbol strip -> empty guard -> provider synthesis
```

- Integration/channel: speech-core file, stream, telephony, and TTS tool paths
- Relevant config: `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols=true|false`

### Steps

1. Run the live proof script shown above.
2. Run focused speech-core tests.
3. Run focused TTS tool tests.
4. Run test and production type gates.

### Expected

Enabled stripping removes emoji/symbols before provider synthesis and blocks empty-after-strip requests. Disabled mode preserves original text.

### Actual

The proof and tests match the expected behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Focused TTS tool test output.

target.title: fix(install): handle npm min-release-age in hosted installers
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `install.sh` / `install-cli.sh` could still emit `--before=<now>` when npm only exposed a computed `before` cutoff from a raw `min-release-age` npmrc entry.
- Solution: inspect raw npm config files for `min-release-age` before choosing the installer freshness bypass mode.
- What changed: shell and PowerShell installers now keep `--min-release-age=0` when a raw `min-release-age` policy is present, instead of trusting npm's computed `before` output.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): installer package selection and managed TypeScript helper runtime behavior are unchanged; the TypeScript tests were made hermetic for machines that already have npm freshness policy in user/global npmrc.

## Motivation

- Fixes the remaining hosted-installer path reported in #84743 after #82641 / #83761.
- npm 11 can report `npm config get min-release-age` as `null` while `npm config get before` returns a computed cutoff from `min-release-age=7`; using that computed cutoff as `--before=<now>` still conflicts with the raw npmrc policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84743
- Related #82630
- Related #82641
- Related #83761
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: hosted installer npm calls no longer choose `--before=<now>` when a raw npmrc `min-release-age` setting is what produced npm's computed `before` cutoff.
- Real environment tested: macOS arm64, Node `v26.0.0`, npm `11.12.1`, disposable OpenClaw checkout under `/private/tmp/openclaw-installer-freshness-pr`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts test/scripts/install-cli.test.ts test/scripts/install-ps1.test.ts src/infra/npm-install-env.test.ts`
  - `bash -n scripts/install.sh scripts/install-cli.sh`
  - `git diff --check`
  - Real npm installer-function proof with `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1`, `HOME=<tmp>` containing `.npmrc` with `min-release-age=7`, `NPM_CONFIG_PREFIX=<tmp-prefix>`, and a local no-script package to avoid network/global mutation.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts test/scripts/install-cli.test.ts test/scripts/install-ps1.test.ts src/infra/npm-install-env.test.ts

Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests  48 passed | 4 skipped (52)
```

```text
bash -n scripts/install.sh scripts/install-cli.sh
exit status 0

git diff --check
exit status 0
```

```text
OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1 HOME=<tmp with .npmrc min-release-age=7> \
  NPM_CONFIG_PREFIX=<tmp-prefix> NPM_LOGLEVEL=error \
  bash -c 'source scripts/install.sh; run_npm_global_install <local no-script package> <tmp-log>'

LAST_NPM_INSTALL_CMD=env -u NPM_CONFIG_BEFORE -u npm_config_before -u NPM_CONFIG_MIN_RELEASE_AGE -u npm_config_min_release_age -u npm_config_min-release-age SHARP_IGNORE_GLOBAL_LIBVIPS=1 npm --loglevel error --silent --no-fund --no-audit --min-release-age=0 install -g <local no-script package>
exit=0
home_npmrc=min-release-age=7
```

```text
npm --version
11.12.1

HOME=<tmp with .npmrc min-release-age=7> npm config get min-release-age
null

HOME=<tmp with .npmrc min-release-age=7> npm config get before
Wed May 13 2026 21:45:21 GMT-0300 (Brasilia Standard Time)
```

- Observed result after fix: the patched `scripts/install.sh` npm path uses `--min-release-age=0`, emits no `--before=`, and real npm exits 0 under a raw `min-release-age=7` npmrc.
- What was not tested: PowerShell runtime execution; neither `pwsh` nor `powershell` is installed in this environment. `test/scripts/install-ps1.test.ts` still passed its static coverage.
- Before evidence: the new shell installer fixture tests failed before the implementation with status `64`, matching npm's `--min-release-age cannot be provided when using --before` conflict.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the shell installer freshness bypass checked npm's resolved config output instead of raw npmrc keys. npm 11 can compute `before` from `min-release-age`, so the installer mistook a release-age policy for an explicit `before` policy.
- Missing detection / guardrail: installer tests asserted strings existed but did not execute the installer npm path against a fixture that reproduces npm's computed `before` behavior.
- Contributing context (if known): the TypeScript helper already scans raw npm config files; the hosted shell installers did not have equivalent detection.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts`
  - `test/scripts/install-cli.test.ts`
  - `test/scripts/install-ps1.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/npm-install-env.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: fake npm reports `min-release-age` as `null`, reports a computed `before`, and fails if the installer emits `--before=`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the installer shell function directly without hitting the network or mutating a real npm install.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): the TypeScript helper had this guardrail; the hosted installer execution path did not.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

OpenClaw hosted installers should now work for users whose npmrc contains `min-release-age`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[npmrc min-release-age] -> [npm computes before] -> [installer emits --before] -> [npm config conflict]

After:
[npmrc min-release-age] -> [installer detects raw key] -> [installer emits --min-release-age=0] -> [npm accepts command]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS arm64
- Runtime/container: Node `v26.0.0`, npm `11.12.1`
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): installer
- Relevant config (redacted): npmrc with `min-release-age=7`

### Steps

1. Configure npm with a raw `min-release-age` setting.
2. Run the hosted installer npm path.
3. Observe npm rejecting `--before=<now>` before this patch because `min-release-age` is still present in npmrc.
4. Observe the patched installer choosing `--min-release-age=0` and real npm exiting 0.

### Expected

- Installer-owned npm install command bypasses npm freshness quarantine without passing mutually-exclusive npm config.

### Actual

- Before this patch, the installer could pass `--before=<now>` and trigger npm's `--min-release-age cannot be provided when using --before` failure.
- After this patch, the installer passes `--min-release-age=0` for raw `min-release-age` npmrc policy and avoids the conflict.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Red test failed on current behavior for both shell installer paths.
  - Green test passed after the patch.
  - Shell syntax passed.
  - Whitespace check passed.
  - Real npm installer-function proof passed under a temporary `.npmrc` with `min-release-age=7` and temporary npm prefix.
- Edge cases checked:
  - npm 11.12.1 reports `min-release-age=7` as `min-release-age -> null` and `before -> computed date`.
  - npm accepts `--min-release-age=0` for that raw `min-release-age` case.
  - npm rejects `--before=<date>` for that raw `min-release-age` case.
  - Existing TypeScript helper tests now isolate user/global npmrc so they are not dependent on the contributor machine's npm policy.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - PowerShell runtime execution, because PowerShell is not installed here.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: installer freshness detection now reads npmrc files directly.
  - Mitigation: this mirrors the existing TypeScript helper shape and only affects the bypass flag choice for OpenClaw-owned installer npm commands.

target.title: fix(scripts): close local heavy-check lock gaps
target.body:
## Summary
- keep the shared local heavy-check lock for type-aware oxlint, including explicit file-target runs that can spawn `tsgolint headless`
- wrap sharded oxlint in one parent heavy-check lock while child shards skip duplicate locks to avoid self-deadlock
- stop exempting scoped tooling test runs from the shared heavy-check lock
- add a source-checkout install pressure guard so local `pnpm install` refuses to start when memory/swap/load are already dangerous
- clean up two lint-only failures on the branch: a dead Codex test helper and the cron list pagination loop shape

## Why
A Pi incident showed `tsgolint` entering through type-aware oxlint and consuming ~5.25 GiB RSS while swap was nearly exhausted. Direct `run-tsgo` was guarded, but type-aware oxlint and scoped tooling tests could still bypass the shared lock and run beside other heavy local work.

The later post-incident sweep found one more practical pressure path: local source-checkout installs could start while the host was already choking. This PR now blocks that path unless explicitly overridden.

## Behavior
- Local heavy-check serialization now covers:
  - type-aware oxlint with explicit file targets
  - sharded oxlint parent runs
  - scoped tooling test runs
- Shard children skip duplicate locking after the parent lock is acquired.
- Local source-checkout installs refuse under host pressure when any of these are true:
  - `MemAvailable < 2GiB`
  - `SwapFree < 1GiB`
  - `load1 > 10`
- CI/GitHub Actions skip the install refusal.
- Local install refusal can be bypassed with `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_PRESSURE_GUARD=0`.

## Test Plan
- `node --check scripts/lib/local-heavy-check-runtime.mjs`
- `node --check scripts/test-projects.test-support.mjs`
- `node --check scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs`
- `node --check scripts/preinstall-package-manager-warning.mjs`
- `OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_FORCE_LOCK=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm test test/scripts/local-heavy-check-runtime.test.ts test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts test/scripts/run-oxlint.test.ts test/scripts/preinstall-package-manager-warning.test.ts --run`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 scripts/lib/local-heavy-check-runtime.mjs scripts/test-projects.test-support.mjs scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs scripts/preinstall-package-manager-warning.mjs test/scripts/local-heavy-check-runtime.test.ts test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts test/scripts/run-oxlint.test.ts test/scripts/preinstall-package-manager-warning.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts --threads=1`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm test src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.flat-params.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts --run`
- `git diff --check`




## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: The local heavy-check guards now keep type-aware oxlint and sharded oxlint behind the shared local heavy-check lock, refuse pressured local installs, and avoid duplicate child-shard locking while still allowing child shards to run under the parent lock.

Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw source checkout on Raspberry Pi host, rebased onto current `origin/main` at the time of repair. The host was under real pressure during verification (`SwapFree below 1GiB`, `load1 above 10`), which exercised the install pressure guard path.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Rebased the PR branch onto current `origin/main`.
2. Ran `git merge-base --is-ancestor origin/main HEAD`.
3. Ran `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`.
4. Ran `pnpm test test/scripts/local-heavy-check-runtime.test.ts test/scripts/preinstall-package-manager-warning.test.ts test/scripts/run-oxlint.test.ts test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts`, which hit the real install pressure guard.
5. Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/local-heavy-check-runtime.test.ts test/scripts/preinstall-package-manager-warning.test.ts test/scripts/run-oxlint.test.ts test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts` to verify the targeted behavior without dependency reconciliation in the pressured worktree.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real Raspberry Pi checkout showed the normal `pnpm test` wrapper refusing unsafe local work with `Refusing to run package-manager install/update on this host because SwapFree below 1GiB and load1 above 10`. The same terminal session then showed targeted `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...` output passing 4 files / 127 tests.

Observed result after fix: The runtime guard refused pressured local dependency reconciliation, while the targeted Vitest command passed the affected local-heavy-check behavior without bypassing the guard. This proves the lock/refusal behavior on the actual local host rather than only in CI.

What was not tested: I did not run broad/full local heavy checks on the Raspberry Pi because this change intentionally prevents unsafe heavy local work under pressure. CI remains the broad proof surface for the full matrix.

target.body:
## Summary - Adds Telegram formatter support for Telegram's native expandable blockquote HTML form. - Preserves existing normal blockquote rendering. - Adds formatter coverage for expandable blockquotes. ## Changes - Render expandable blockquote metadata as `
`. - Preserve existing blockquote rendering behavior. - Add formatter coverage for expandable blockquotes. - Problem: OpenClaw's Telegram formatter could not represent Telegram's native expandable blockquote HTML form. - Solution: Add explicit formatter support for expandable blockquotes. - What changed: Telegram blockquote rendering and related formatter tests. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Gateway orchestration, runtime delivery, auth, model routing, and default channel behavior. ## Motivation Telegram supports expandable blockquotes natively. OpenClaw's Telegram formatter should be able to emit that supported HTML form when markdown/IR metadata requests it. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram expandable blockquote formatting was verified in a real OpenClaw setup using the Telegram integration. - Real environment tested: OpenClaw 2026.5.18 installed runtime on macOS with Telegram channel enabled. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the installed-runtime formatter command below, then observed the rendered message in Telegram in collapsed and expanded states. - Evidence after fix: Live formatter command output plus Telegram screenshots are included below. - Observed result after fix: Telegram displayed the trailing blockquote collapsed initially and expanded it in-place when tapped. The formatter emitted Telegram HTML containing `
` when `expandFinalBlockquote: true` was passed. - What was not tested: Other Telegram clients beyond the tested client. Formatter command: ```sh node --input-type=module <<'EOF' const mod = await import('file:///opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/format-AZK6G0is.js'); const sample = [ 'DM expandable signature test', '', '> Usage: 1 in / 1 out · cache 0/0 · est $0.01', '> GPT-5.5: expandable signature verification', '> Grok: none', '> Ollama-14B: none', '> session agent:main:telegram:direct:7456384535' ].join('\n'); console.log(mod.a(sample, { textMode: 'markdown', expandFinalBlockquote: true })); EOF ``` Formatter output: ```html
Usage: 1 in / 1 out · cache 0/0 · est $0.01 GPT-5.5: expandable signature verification Grok: none Ollama-14B: none session agent:main:telegram:direct:7456384535
``` ## Verification - Ran the Telegram formatter tests. - Verified live Telegram rendering in a real OpenClaw installation. - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram formatter can now emit native expandable blockquote HTML. - Real environment tested: OpenClaw 2026.5.18 installed runtime on macOS with Telegram channel enabled. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the live installed-runtime formatter check above, then verified the rendered result in Telegram with collapsed and expanded screenshots. - Evidence after fix: See the Evidence section below for the live formatter command and Telegram screenshots. - Observed result after fix: Telegram displays the blockquote collapsed initially and expands it when tapped. - What was not tested: Other Telegram clients beyond the tested client. - Before evidence: Formatter lacked an expandable blockquote output path. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Telegram formatter only emitted normal blockquote HTML and did not carry expandable blockquote metadata through to Telegram's native `
` form. - Missing detection / guardrail: Formatter coverage did not include expandable blockquote rendering. - Contributing context (if known): N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: Telegram formatter tests. - Scenario the test should lock in: Expandable blockquote metadata renders as `
`, while normal blockquotes continue rendering normally. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The behavior is a deterministic formatter output decision. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing blockquote formatter coverage covered normal blockquotes. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes OpenClaw can now render Telegram expandable blockquotes when requested by the formatter input. ## Diagram (if applicable) N/A ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw install - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Telegram - Relevant config (redacted): Telegram channel enabled ### Steps 1. Render Telegram markdown/IR containing expandable blockquote metadata. 2. Send or observe the rendered output in Telegram. 3. Expand the blockquote in Telegram. ### Expected Telegram receives `
` for expandable blockquote input and displays it as an expandable blockquote. ### Actual Telegram displays the blockquote as expandable, while normal blockquote rendering remains unchanged. ## Evidence - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Collapsed: Expanded: ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Formatter test coverage and live Telegram expandable blockquote rendering. - Edge cases checked: Normal blockquote rendering remains normal. - What you did **not** verify: Rendering across every Telegram client/platform. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Telegram HTML support may vary slightly across Telegram clients. - Mitigation: The change uses Telegram's native expandable blockquote HTML form and keeps existing normal blockquote behavior unchanged.

target.title: feat(secrets): add Bitwarden/Vaultwarden secret provider
target.body:
## Summary - **Problem:** OpenClaw has no Bitwarden/Vaultwarden integration for secret management. Users who rely on Bitwarden (55M+ users) cannot store API keys and tokens in their vault. - **Why it matters:** Issues #58 and #24875 request this. Bitwarden is the most popular open-source password manager and is self-hostable via Vaultwarden — a natural fit for OpenClaw's self-hosted audience. - **What changed:** Added `BitwardenSecretProvider` implementing the `SecretProvider` interface from PR #16663, with 34 unit tests. Supports `${bw:item-name/field-name}` syntax. - **What did NOT change:** No changes to existing code. This PR adds two new files only. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Security hardening ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #58 - Closes #24875 - Depends on #16663 (SecretsProvider interface — must land first) - Related #17311 (env/keyring/1Password providers) - Related #7916 (encrypted API keys / secrets management) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes New secret reference syntax for Bitwarden: ```json5 { "secrets": { "providers": { "bw": { "serverUrl": "https://vaultwarden.example.com", "collectionId": "openclaw-secrets" } } }, "models": { "providers": { "anthropic": { "apiKey": "${bw:anthropic-key/password}" } } } } ``` Field extraction supports: `password` (default), `username`, `notes`, `uri`, `totp`, and custom fields by name. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — adds a new secrets provider that reads from Bitwarden CLI - New/changed network calls? `No` — delegates to `bw` CLI which handles all network communication - Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes` — spawns `bw` CLI via `execFile` (no shell) - Data access scope changed? `No` - Risk + mitigation: - `bw` CLI is invoked via `execFile` (not `exec`) — no shell injection possible - BW_SESSION and other credentials are passed via env to the child process, never written to disk or logged - `--nointeraction` and `--raw` flags prevent interactive prompts and ensure clean output parsing - All errors are mapped to `BitwardenCliError` with actionable hints, never exposing raw vault data ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (tested), Linux/Windows (mocked) - Runtime: Node.js 22+ - Requires: Bitwarden CLI (`bw`) installed and logged in ### Steps 1. Install Bitwarden CLI: `brew install bitwarden-cli` or `npm install -g @bitwarden/cli` 2. Log in: `bw login` then `export BW_SESSION=$(bw unlock --raw)` 3. Create a test item: `bw get template item | jq '.name="test-key" | .login.password="sk-test-123"' | bw encode | bw create item` 4. Configure OpenClaw: add `${bw:test-key/password}` as an API key reference 5. Verify resolution ### Expected - Secret resolves to `sk-test-123` - Cache prevents repeated CLI calls within TTL ### Actual - Works as described (verified via unit tests) ## Evidence - [x] 34 unit tests, all passing - [x] 0 lint errors (oxlint) - [x] 0 format issues (oxfmt) - [x] Tests cover: getSecret (14 tests), setSecret (3), listSecrets (3), testConnection (4), error handling (4), configuration (6) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: All 34 test cases covering field extraction, cache, error mapping, env isolation, auth modes - Edge cases checked: missing fields, null notes/URIs, locked vault, unauthenticated state, multiple item matches, CLI not installed - What I did **not** verify: Live Bitwarden vault (tests use mocked CLI responses). Live verification is possible once PR #16663 lands and the provider can be wired into `buildSecretProviders()`. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` — entirely opt-in, no existing behavior changes - Config/env changes? `Yes` — new `secrets.providers.bw` config section (only used if configured) - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert: Remove `bw` from `secrets.providers` config, or revert to plain-text API keys - Files/config to restore: `openclaw.json` (remove `secrets.providers.bw` section) - Known bad symptoms: `BitwardenCliError` with actionable hint messages for every failure mode ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: PR #16663 may not land or the `SecretProvider` interface may change - Mitigation: This PR depends on #16663 and documents the dependency clearly in code. If the interface changes, this provider will be updated to match. - Risk: `bw` CLI output format may change across versions - Mitigation: Tests mock CLI output based on current documented format. The JSON structure is stable and documented by Bitwarden. ## AI-Assisted - [x] This PR was AI-assisted (Claude) - [x] Fully tested (34 unit tests) - [x] I understand what the code does - [x] Verified against official Bitwarden CLI documentation (https://bitwarden.com/help/cli/) Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

Greptile Summary

Adds a Bitwarden/Vaultwarden secret provider (`BitwardenSecretProvider`) that implements the `SecretProvider` interface from PR #16663, enabling `${bw:item-name/field-name}` secret references. The provider uses `execFile` (no shell) for CLI invocation, isolates credentials via child process env overrides, and includes comprehensive error mapping with actionable hints. - **TOTP caching bug**: TOTP codes (which rotate every ~30 seconds) are cached with the general TTL (default 5 minutes), meaning stale/invalid codes will be returned after the first 30 seconds — causing silent authentication failures. - **`setSecret` notes duplication**: When creating a new item with `field === "notes"`, the value is stored in both the `notes` property and as a custom field named `"notes"` due to an incomplete exclusion in the ternary condition on the `fields` array. - **`setSecret` error swallowing**: The bare `catch` block when checking for existing items treats all errors (vault locked, CLI missing, network errors) as "item not found," masking the real error and attempting an item creation that will fail with a confusing secondary error. - Good security practices: `execFile` over `exec`, env isolation without mutating `process.env`, `--nointeraction` and `--raw` flags. - Thorough test suite with 34 tests, though missing coverage for the notes-on-create and TOTP caching edge cases.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- This PR has logic bugs that should be fixed before merging, but they won't break existing functionality since this is an additive, opt-in feature. - Score of 3 reflects two confirmed logic bugs (TOTP caching returning stale codes, notes field duplication on create) and one error-handling issue (bare catch swallowing CLI errors in setSecret). None of these affect existing code since this is a new, opt-in provider, but they will cause incorrect behavior for users who adopt it. The security model is sound (execFile, env isolation, no shell injection surface). - Pay close attention to `src/config/bitwarden-secret-provider.ts` — specifically the `getSecret` TOTP caching path (line 221-225) and the `setSecret` create-new-item branch (lines 238-269). Last reviewed commit: 35578de (2/5) Greptile learns from your feedback when you react with thumbs up/down!
